Title: Projects — the multi-project layer
Summary: Bleepforge holds many projects; one is active at a time. Three creation modes (notebook / sync / import-once), switch via /projects with instant in-process hot-reload, project state lives at projects/<slug>/.
Body:
## One install, many projects

As of v0.2.5 a single Bleepforge install can hold any number of projects. Exactly one is **active** at a time — that's whose data shows up across every domain page. The active-project chip in the sidebar (under the BLEEPFORGE wordmark) always tells you which one you're looking at; click it to open [Projects](/projects) and switch.

Projects live on disk under `<bleepforgeRoot>/projects/<slug>/`:

- `data/` — Bleepforge's JSON cache + Bleepforge-only authored content (concept, codex, dialog layouts, shader metadata).
- `content/` — notebook-mode projects' assets + shaders (PNGs, .gdshader files). Sync-mode projects skip this dir; their content lives in the Godot tree.

`bleepforgeRoot` is `dialoguer/` in dev mode, `~/.config/Bleepforge/` (Linux) / `~/Library/Application Support/Bleepforge/` (macOS) / `%APPDATA%/Bleepforge/` (Windows) when running the packaged app.

## Three creation modes

**Notebook** — Standalone. Bleepforge owns the data + assets + shaders inside the project. No Godot connection. Use for design docs, project notebooks, or any project that won't ship through Godot.

**Sync to Godot** — Two-way live sync with a Godot project's `.tres` files. Bleepforge mirrors the entity JSON from `.tres` on every boot and writes edits back on save; the watcher catches Godot-side changes live. Pick this when you have a real Godot project and want both sides to stay in lockstep.

**Import once from Godot** — Snapshot a Godot project's content into a fresh notebook. The `.tres` data lands as JSON, referenced images + shaders get copied into the new project's `content/` dir, and refs are rewritten to the portable `content://` scheme. After the snapshot completes the project is independent — edits never touch the source Godot tree. Use this when you want to fork a project for safe exploration, or grab a snapshot for archival.

The **stored** mode on a Project record is either `sync` or `notebook`. Import-once is a creation-flow flag — the resulting project is `notebook` mode after the seed completes.

## Path portability — content://

Notebook + import-once projects use a `content://` URL scheme for asset references in JSON: `content://images/foo.png` resolves to `<contentRoot>/images/foo.png` at request time. This makes the project portable — move the Bleepforge install to another machine and the refs still resolve, as long as the project's `content/` dir comes with it.

Sync mode keeps storing absolute paths for now. Migrating existing data to `content://` is wider work than the v0.2.5 cut allowed; new notebook projects start portable from day one.

## Switching projects is instant — no restart

The active project's paths (data root, content root, Godot root, mode) hot-reload in place on switch. `POST /api/projects/reload` re-reads the active pointer + project record and rebuilds the in-process state — watcher, caches, project index, optional reconcile, pending-import seed if any — against the new active project. ~15ms on FoB.

This works identically in dev mode (`pnpm dev:desktop`) and packaged AppImage. No window flash, no Electron / dev-server lifecycle to wait on.

The sidebar's project chip + the /projects page distinguish two states cleanly:

- **Active** (emerald) — what the server is currently serving.
- **Queued** (amber) — what's written on disk but not yet picked up by the server. Should only show briefly between an action that flips the pointer and the auto-reload that follows. If a queued indicator gets stuck, click the sidebar's restart icon (it'll glow amber in that state) to fire the reload manually.

## Rename + delete

The **"⋯"** menu on each project card (and right-click on the card itself) opens two actions:

- **Rename** — change the display name. The slug stays immutable because it's the on-disk directory name AND the active-pointer key; renaming a slug would require renaming the dir AND fixing every reference to it.
- **Delete** — pick **Forget** to remove the project from Bleepforge but keep its files at `projects/<slug>/` (you can re-register it later by re-importing from the dir), or **Delete files** to also `rm -rf` that directory.

For sync-mode projects: deletion never touches the Godot tree itself. Bleepforge only deletes what it owns. Your Godot project on disk is untouched, regardless of which delete option you pick.

You can't delete the **active** project (switch first) or the **last** project (create a new one first). Both refusals return clear 409 errors at the API layer.

## Migration from v0.2.4 and earlier

On first boot of v0.2.5+, if the legacy `data/` dir has content, Bleepforge migrates it automatically:

1. Slug is derived from `concept.json`'s Title (fallback `flock-of-bleeps`).
2. `data/` contents are `mv`'d into `projects/<slug>/data/`.
3. The old `godotProjectRoot` value from `preferences.json` is pulled into the new project record.
4. `projects.json` (registry) + `active-project.json` (pointer) are written at the Bleepforge root.
5. The legacy `data/` dir is left empty as a verification window — remove it manually after confirming the migration looks right.

> tip: see [Project root](/help/getting-started/project-root) for the older single-project setup story. The Preferences-page Godot field still works for sync-mode projects (it writes through to the active project's record), but it's no longer the canonical source — the project record is.
